ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese stir-fried beef vermicelli; broader noodle dish term used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (stir-fried noodle dish with meat) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 category; no exact code exists for Vietnamese beef vermicelli stir-fry.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of stir-fried sliced beef vermicelli weighs approximately 450 g, representing a typical single-person restaurant serving as listed on the GrabFood menu.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Noodles, rice, cooked (FDC ID 168936) and Beef, ground, 85% lean / 15% fat, cooked (FDC ID 174036); composite estimate weighted by typical recipe proportions for Vietnamese stir-fried beef vermicelli
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for Vietnamese stir-fried beef vermicelli as a composite dish. Values were derived by weighting USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked rice vermicelli (FDC ID 168936, ~50% by weight), lean beef (FDC ID 174036, ~25%), vegetables (bean sprouts, carrot, onion ~20%), and sauce/oil (~5%), then cross-checked against the input record values (provenance: GrabFood, confidence 0.7). The input per_100g values are within plausible range for this dish composition and have been retained with medium confidence. Singapore HPB Foodo database was also consulted but does not contain a direct entry for this Vietnamese dish. The input source listing of https://foodo.sg/ was reviewed but does not directly support the nutrient values for this specific dish; USDA FoodData Central is used as the primary approved nutrient reference per Project Veritas source policy.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — rice vermicelli (GI 53, glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 53 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database for rice vermicelli (boiled), which is the primary carbohydrate component of this dish. Per the standard GI classification scale (low: ≤55, medium: 56–69, high: ≥70), a GI of 53 falls in the low category. The overall dish GI is estimated as low (53) because the beef, vegetables, and sauce components do not substantially alter the GI of the noodle base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (53) × available carbohydrate per serving (450 g × 14.4 g/100 g = 64.8 g) ÷ 100 = 34.3, rounded to 34.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150–200 g and request reduced soy sauce, fish sauce, and oyster sauce to bring per-serving sodium below 600 mg; potassium intake from this dish is insufficient to offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice vermicelli has a low GI of 53; however, a full 450 g plate delivers a glycemic load of approximately 34, which is high and will produce a significant postprandial glucose rise if the full portion is consumed.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, pair the dish with additional non-starchy vegetables to increase fibre and slow glucose absorption, and avoid adding extra sauce to keep sugar and sodium intake controlled.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium is high at 400 mg/100 g and saturated fat is 1.8 g/100 g; a full serving contributes approximately 1800 mg sodium and 8.1 g saturated fat, both of which are relevant cardiovascular risk factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request a reduced-sauce preparation to lower sodium, choose a smaller portion (150–200 g), and consider substituting beef with a leaner protein such as chicken breast or tofu to reduce saturated fat and arachidonic acid intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid), which amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6); high-heat stir-frying also generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise this dish due to the pro-inflammatory beef component and AGE generation from high-heat cooking; substituting beef with tofu or chicken and requesting lower-heat preparation would substantially reduce inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Sliced Beef Vermicelli (越南干炒牛肉米粉; Vietnamese: Bún Xào Thịt Bò) is a Vietnamese-origin noodle dish widely served in Singapore's Vietnamese restaurant segment. The dish consists of thin rice vermicelli noodles (bún) stir-fried at high heat in a wok with thinly sliced beef, bean sprouts, julienned carrot, onion, garlic, and a seasoning blend of soy sauce, oyster sauce, fish sauce, and black pepper. The cooking technique is dry stir-frying (干炒), which produces lightly charred, chewy noodles with a savoury, umami-rich flavour profile and a slightly smoky wok aroma (wok hei). The texture contrasts tender beef slices with crisp bean sprouts and soft noodles. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 144 kcal, 7.8 g protein, 6.2 g fat, and 14.4 g carbohydrate, with sodium at 400 mg — a clinically significant level. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low category per standard GI scale ≤55) based on the rice vermicelli base; a full 450 g plate delivers a glycemic load of approximately 34, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is not suitable for hypertension without sauce reduction, and the red meat content makes it a concern for rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease management. It is dairy-free but contains soy, gluten (from oyster sauce), fish, and shellfish allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central) contains an exact entry for Vietnamese stir-fried beef vermicelli as a composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated by weighting USDA FoodData Central component entries (cooked rice vermicelli FDC ID 168936, lean beef FDC ID 174036, vegetables, sauce) and cross-validated against the input GrabFood-sourced values (confidence 0.7). The input values are plausible and internally consistent; medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin profile, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese stir-fried dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. 2. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.1 g/100 g from the input source; this is a trace level consistent with small amounts of trans fat from beef (naturally occurring conjugated linoleic acid) and cooking oil. 3. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli (boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database; per the standard GI classification scale (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), GI 53 is classified as low. The gi_category has been corrected from 'medium' to 'low' to match GI 53. The composite dish GI may vary depending on sauce volume and cooking method. 4. Halal status is marked false as oyster sauce and the specific beef sourcing cannot be confirmed as halal-certified without restaurant-level verification. 5. The input source listing of https://foodo.sg/ was reviewed but does not directly support the nutrient values for this specific dish; USDA FoodData Central (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/) is used as the primary approved nutrient reference per Project Veritas source policy. The input nutrient values (provenance: GrabFood, confidence 0.7) were cross-validated against the USDA composite estimate and found to be within plausible range. 6. Aliases self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array — confirmed consistent.

All four GI fields present and sourced. GI category 'low' is now consistent with GI 53.
